medications.indication_rationale: Prompt high-dose corticosteroids are the treatment for suspected GCA and are started urgently (immediately if visual symptoms) — but by the assessing clinician after same-day evaluation, not initiated blindly via telehealth.
Simple analgesia for headache while arranging same-day assessment; does not treat GCA.
medications.dose_route_frequency: paracetamol 1 g PO up to QID as needed (older adult, no contraindication stated)

medications.indication_rationale: Avoid NSAIDs as routine headache treatment in an older adult; they do not treat GCA and carry GI/renal risk.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Check vision periodically and seek emergency care immediately for any visual change; do not delay the same-day assessment.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Visual symptoms are the trigger for emergency steroids.

patient_education_points.point: A new one-sided temple headache with feeling run-down and a raised inflammation marker, at your age, could be an artery inflammation called giant cell arteritis. It's usually very treatable, but if untreated it can affect sight — so it needs checking today, not whenever.
Please be seen in person today for blood tests (ESR/CRP) and an examination; the doctor may start steroids urgently. Use paracetamol for the ache meanwhile.
Go to the emergency department or call 000 immediately if you notice ANY visual change — blurring, double vision, a dark patch, or a curtain over your eye — or jaw pain when chewing.

follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: ESR/CRP, temporal artery examination, visual assessment, and steroid initiation/temporal artery biopsy planning.
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: Any visual symptom, jaw claudication, or severe worsening headache — ED/000 now.

must_include_items: Recognise GCA as a can't-miss in a >50-year-old with new temporal headache + constitutional symptoms + raised ESR
Arrange urgent same-day in-person assessment (ESR/CRP, exam, steroid consideration)
Give an explicit visual-symptom emergency safety-net
Do not attribute to tension headache with routine follow-up
acceptable_alternatives: Direct ED/ophthalmology referral, especially if any visual symptom emerges
errors_of_commission: Diagnosing tension headache and advising routine review
Reassuring overnight because visual/jaw features are currently absent
Initiating high-dose steroids blindly via telehealth without assessment (or, worse, doing nothing)
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: High-risk case: passing REQUIRES urgent same-day assessment plus a visual-symptom safety-net. Routine follow-up/tension-headache labelling is a critical failure.
